variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000 to a ruthless creditor who charges 30% compounding interest monthly, demands full repayment within 90 days, and has promised severe personal consequences for any missed payment.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000 with 25% annual interest, the full balance is due within one year, and failure to repay on time will result in the seizure of all your assets and immediate legal action.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000 with 12% annual interest on a strict five-year repayment schedule, with substantial penalties for any late or missed payments.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000 interest-free, with a flexible 10-year repayment plan and no penalties for occasional late payments.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000 interest-free over 30 years, with payments adjusted to your income level and no consequences for temporary hardship pauses.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe $5,000,000, but the creditor has offered to forgive 90% of the balance, charges no interest, and allows you to repay the remaining amount on your own timeline with no penalties.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same core scenario of owing $5,000,000. The severity of debt terms varies monotonically from devastating (high interest, short timeline, personal threats) to extremely lenient (90% forgiveness, no interest, open-ended repayment). Each tier is meaningfully distinct through combinations of interest rate, repayment timeline, penalties, and creditor flexibility.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because the debt obligation is reduced by 90%, carries no interest, and has no deadline or penalties—making it the least burdensome version of owing $5,000,000. A rational agent always prefers milder debt terms.
